nativeRange.habitat: Coastal coral and rocky reefs, often in silty or turbid lagoons; midwater schools typically at 1–30 m, recorded to 60 m

decorPreferences: live rock perimeter with caves and crevices

diet.notes: In the wild this species feeds primarily on zooplankton in midwater. In captivity, has been documented accepting frozen mysis shrimp, enriched frozen brine shrimp, krill, clam meat, and high-quality prepared meaty foods. Multiple sources describe this fish as a constant grazer; small portions fed four to six times per day are generally advised to match the species' high metabolism.

goodWith: reef fish of similar size and temperament

summary: The Redbelly Yellowtail Fusilier is a fast-swimming, schooling reef fish from Indo-Pacific coastal waters that is generally considered peaceful but grows to around 60 cm and requires very large aquariums with ample horizontal swimming room.
careNotes:
The Redbelly Yellowtail Fusilier (Caesio cuning) is a midwater schooling species from coastal Indo-Pacific reefs. Adult coloration is silvery blue with a pink to reddish flush on the belly, a yellow caudal fin, and a small black spot at the base of the pectoral fin. Juveniles offered for sale are typically much smaller than the documented maximum adult size of 60 cm, but planning around fully grown dimensions is generally advised, as requirements will grow as fish mature.

This species tends to need very large aquariums — a minimum tank length of around 300 cm is recommended — with robust water flow and substantial open swimming space in the mid-water column. While water-quality sensitivity is not exceptional for a reef fish, the combination of adult size, constant-feeding requirements, and schooling behavior means the practical demands of keeping this species tend toward the advanced end of intermediate care; tank capacity and feeding logistics are the main limiting factors.

Caesio cuning has been reported to school in nature, and keeping at least three individuals together is generally advised in captivity. Reef compatibility is typically listed as 'with caution': it is not known to nip corals, but its size and active swimming can disturb sessile invertebrates and stress smaller or more timid tankmates. Small ornamental shrimp may be at risk.

This species has been documented jumping from open aquaria; a tightly fitted lid is recommended.
breedingNotes: Reported as oviparous in the wild, releasing numerous small pelagic eggs in midwater. Captive breeding has been mentioned as possible but is not common, and rearing larvae is reported to be impractical at hobby scale; captive supply is essentially all wild-caught.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary source for tank size (300 gal), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), temperament, care level, and reef compatibility.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed scientific name, family (Caesionidae), Indo-West Pacific native range (Sri Lanka to Vanuatu; southern Japan to northern Australia), maximum total length 60 cm, depth range 1–60 m, planktivorous diet, oviparous reproduction, wild temperature 26.1–29.1°C.

additional.name: Reeflex
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (50–60 cm), Indo-Pacific range, and aquarium recommendation of approximately 3000 L for a school; rates care difficulty as advanced. Used to bias minimum tank volume upward.

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (~24 in / 60 cm), minimum tank (250 gal / 946 L), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), peaceful temperament, schooling behavior, and constant-grazing feeding pattern (~6 feedings per day).

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous broadcast spawner in the wild; captive breeding has been reported as possible but is not at scale, and larval rearing is not practical at hobby level.
